SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper provides empirical evaluation on a large adjective–noun dataset (Gutiérrez et al., 2016) and compares to strong baselines including the supervised and semi‑supervised tensor methods and cosine similarity, supporting the core claim of competitive performance. However, no ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported, hyperparameters and code are not provided (hindering reproducibility), and failure cases or limitations are not discussed. Related work coverage is said to be thorough, but the missing methodological details and lack of deeper analyses leave important gaps.

IMPACT.justification: Addressing zero-shot identification of novel adjective–noun metaphors is an important problem in NLP, and the paper’s geometric, context-sensitive method that builds ad hoc subspaces per word pair directly targets this challenge. Demonstrating comparable F-scores to a supervised tensor method (Gutiérrez et al., 2016) while using only individual word vectors and a simple logistic classifier suggests a shift away from expensive supervised/tensor approaches for this task. The approach’s simplicity and generality (PMI-based space, per-pair projections) are likely to inspire follow-up work and could be applied to other compositional or context-sensitive semantic tasks, so the contribution has strong but not necessarily transformative impact given the evaluation scope is limited to one adjective–noun dataset.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes a notable new perspective—a geometric, context-sensitive model that constructs an ad hoc subspace per adjective–noun pair to enable zero-shot metaphor identification using only individual word vectors—which is a clear departure from supervised tensor approaches. It leverages known components (PMI-based vectors, McGregor et al. 2015 subspace selection, logistic regression), so the techniques are a non-trivial but not radical extension of existing methods. By explicitly contrasting its zero-shot, vector-only approach with Gutiérrez et al. (2016) and reporting comparable F-scores, the contribution is clearly differentiated and likely to be informative to experts.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This work addresses a core NLP problem (metaphor identification) using distributional semantics and a zero-shot geometric approach, which is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. It targets the right audience (lexical semantics, representation learning, and metaphor detection) and compares against appropriate baselines on a standard dataset (Gutiérrez et al. 2016) with standard metrics (F-score). The methodology (PMI-based space from English Wikipedia, per-pair subspace projection, logistic regression) and evaluation setup match the expectations for a top-tier conference contribution and is well positioned within the community.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well-organized and the main contributions are clearly stated early; the methodology and experiments are described logically (construction of PMI space, contextual subspace projection, logistic regression) and the writing quality is noted as good with thorough related work coverage. Minor presentation issues reduce clarity for reproducibility: hyperparameters are not reported, code availability is not mentioned, and no limitations are stated. Overall, the manuscript communicates its ideas clearly but would benefit from adding those missing experimental details.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against the directly relevant Gutiérrez et al. (2016) supervised and semi‑supervised tensor methods and includes reasonable baselines (word2vec cosine + logistic, majority) on the same Gutiérrez et al. dataset using F‑score, and reports when prior methods perform comparably (supervised tensor) or worse (semi‑supervised). Differences in approach (geometric ad‑hoc subspaces vs. tensor/phrase representations) are described and related work coverage is noted as thorough. Minor omissions (e.g., no hyperparameter/code reporting) slightly weaken reproducibility and fairness assessment, so the comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states clear empirical claims and supports them with experiments on the Gutiérrez et al. (2016) adjective–noun dataset and appropriate baselines (supervised and semi‑supervised tensor methods, cosine similarity, majority). However, key methodological details are missing from the summary (hyperparameters not reported, no mention of statistical significance tests or data splits, code not available), which weakens reproducibility and confidence in the reported F‑scores. There is also a potential logical gap: the model is described as a "zero‑shot" method yet uses logistic regression for classification, and the summary does not clarify how training/zero‑shot evaluation are separated.

main_claims: Proposes a geometric, context-sensitive model that generates an ad hoc subspace for each candidate adjective-noun pair to identify metaphors.
Model requires only individual word vectors (no phrase representations), enabling a zero-shot method capable of identifying novel, unseen metaphors.
Reports F-scores comparable to the supervised tensor method of Gutiérrez et al. (2016) and superior to their semi-supervised method on the same evaluation dataset.
Model is simple and general: it projects word pairs into interpretable geometric relationships and classifies these subspaces using logistic regression.
_summary.methodology: Construct a PMI-based distributional vector space from the December 2014 English Wikipedia, select k-dimensional subspaces per adjective-noun pair based on independent and correlated properties of the two word vectors (following McGregor et al., 2015), project the pair into this contextual subspace, and classify metaphoricity via logistic regression.
datasets: Gutiérrez et al. (2016) adjective-noun dataset (8,592 dyads)
December 2014 English Wikipedia (used to compute PMI co-occurrence statistics)
baselines: Gutiérrez et al. (2016) supervised adjective-tensor method
Gutiérrez et al. (2016) semi-supervised adjective-tensor method
Logistic regression on word2vec cosine similarity between adjectives and nouns
